coredata.dc:title: All-solid-state Z-scheme system of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 for visible light-driven efficient overall water splitting
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Chang'an
Fu, Yijun
Zhao, Juan
Wang, Huibo
Huang, Hui
Liu, Yang
Dou, Yujiang
Shao, Mingwang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: Solar light driven photocatalytic water splitting to produce hydrogen (H2) and oxygen (O2) on large scale is a promising solution to acquire the clean and sustainable energy sources. Enlightened by mimicking natural Z-scheme photosynthesis, the artificial visible light-driven water splitting photocatalyst of NiO/carbon dots/BiVO4 (NiO/CDs/BiVO4) composite was constructed. The designed NiO/CDs/BiVO4 as all-solid-state Z-scheme photocatalyst exhibits efficient photocatalyatic activity for overall water splitting without any cocatalysts or scavengers. In the Z-scheme system, the NiO and BiVO4 can respectively act as H2 evolution and O2 evolution photocatalysts, while the CDs can serve as solid electron mediator. The optimal NiO/CDs/BiVO4 photocatalyst shows the H2 and O2 production rates of 1.21 and 0.60 μmol·h−1, respectively, with the CDs concentration in the composite of 5 wt% and the mass ratio of NiO to BiVO4 of 3. The incorporation of CDs employed as the solid-state electron mediator boosts the effective electron transfer and charges separation, resulting in the improved photocatalytic activity of the designed Z-scheme system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Z-scheme photocatalyst
Carbon dots
All-solid-state
Solid-state electron mediator
Overall water splitting

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Study on the photocatalytic mechanism and process

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of NiO (black curve), BiVO4 (blue curve) and NiO/CDs/BiVO4 (red curve). (b) FTIR and (c) Raman spectra of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 (red curves), NiO (black curves), CDs (pink curves), and BiVO4 (blue curves), respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
TEM images and HRTEM images of (a, d) NiO, (b, e) BiVO4 and (c, f) NiO/CDs/BiVO4, respectively.
(a) XPS survey spectrum and high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s, (d) Ni 2p, (e) Bi 4f and (f) V 2p for NiO/CDs/BiVO4.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of NiO, BiVO4 and NiO/CDs/BiVO4. The inset picture is the Tauc plots of NiO and BiVO4, respectively. (b) and (c) UPS spectra of NiO and BiVO4. (d) The band structure diagram of NiO and BiVO4.

(a) Time dependence of H2 and O2 evolution from ultrapure water under visible light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm), catalyzed by NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites. (b) The cycling stability of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 for water splitting under visible light. (c) Amount of H2 and O2 evolutions of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 with different concentrations of CDs in the complex, where the mass ratio of NiO-BiVO4 is maintained as 3. (d) The H2 and O2 evolutions of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 with different mass ratio of NiO-BiVO4, while the CDs concentration is maintained to be 0.05 gCDs/gcatalyst in the complex.

(a) The current–time curves of NiO, BiVO4 and NiO/CDs/BiVO4 electrochemical activity under visible light (λ ≥ 420 nm). (b) Electrochemical impedance spectra of NiO, BiVO4 and NiO/CDs/BiVO4 and the equivalent circuit model for EIS measurements. (c) PL spectra of NiO, BiVO4 and NiO/CDs/BiVO4, respectively. (d) The RRDE collection experiment for NiO/CDs/BiVO4 with a rotating speed of 1600 rpm.
Schematic model for the overall water splitting process of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 under visible light.

head.ce:title: All-solid-state Z-scheme system of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 for visible light-driven efficient overall water splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University, 199 Ren'ai Road, Suzhou 215123, Jiangsu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices
Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electronic and Information Engineering, Soochow University, 1 Shizi Street, Suzhou 215123, Jiangsu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electronic and Information Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The all-solid-state Z-scheme NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites show efficient photocatalyatic activity for overall water splitting without any cocatalysts or scavengers. The favorable photocatalytic activity of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites should attribute to the effective separation and transfer of photo-induced charge carriers with addition of CDs, which largely restrain the recombination rate of electrons and holes.

Solar light driven photocatalytic water splitting to produce hydrogen (H2) and oxygen (O2) on large scale is a promising solution to acquire the clean and sustainable energy sources. Enlightened by mimicking natural Z-scheme photosynthesis, the artificial visible light-driven water splitting photocatalyst of NiO/carbon dots/BiVO4 (NiO/CDs/BiVO4) composite was constructed. The designed NiO/CDs/BiVO4 as all-solid-state Z-scheme photocatalyst exhibits efficient photocatalyatic activity for overall water splitting without any cocatalysts or scavengers. In the Z-scheme system, the NiO and BiVO4 can respectively act as H2 evolution and O2 evolution photocatalysts, while the CDs can serve as solid electron mediator. The optimal NiO/CDs/BiVO4 photocatalyst shows the H2 and O2 production rates of 1.21 and 0.60 μmol·h−1, respectively, with the CDs concentration in the composite of 5 wt% and the mass ratio of NiO to BiVO4 of 3. The incorporation of CDs employed as the solid-state electron mediator boosts the effective electron transfer and charges separation, resulting in the improved photocatalytic activity of the designed Z-scheme system.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar light driven photocatalytic water splitting to produce hydrogen (H2) and oxygen (O2) on large scale is a promising pathway to acquire the clean and sustainable energy sources [1]. At present, numerous photoactive semiconductors have been utilized for solar water splitting [2–6]. However, the activity of single-phase semiconductor photocatalyst is greatly limited by its insufficient light absorption and fast recombination of charge carriers. Additionally, the conventional heterojunctions typically result in unfavorable losses of redox ability for photogenerated charge carriers [7–9]. Inspired by mimicking natural Z-scheme photosynthesis, the artificial water splitting photocatalysts have attracted infinite attention. The artificial Z-scheme system constitutes of two types of photocatalysts with different band structures, one acting as PSI for H2 evolution and the other as PSII for O2 evolution. Up to now, various Z-scheme systems have been designed, such as CdS-Au-TiO2 [10], ZnRh2O4-Ag-Ag1−xSbO3−y [11], (Ru/SrTiO3:Rh)-(Fe3+/Fe2+)-(BiVO4) [12]. In the current Z-scheme systems, the noble metal (Ag, Au) or redox pair (Fe3+/Fe2+) were usually used as mediators, leading to relatively high cost and low stability of these photocatalysts for the practical application. As described above, a cost-efficient solid electron mediator plays an essential role in relaying the electron to improve the catalytic efficiency of the Z-scheme system and recover the photocatalysts [13]. Therefore, the demonstration of a cost-effective and efficient Z-scheme photocatalytic system with stable solid state mediator, high charge separation efficiency and long-term durability for visible-light-driven overall water splitting is imperative.

P-type nano-structured nickel oxide (NiO) has been ordinarily employed as cocatalyst for the enhancement of photocatalytic activity for water splitting in many researches [14–17]. In addition, the NiO with band gap energy in the range of 3.5–4.0 eV [15] can accomplish the water splitting to produce H2 in theory. However, large band gap of NiO leads to the unavailability of visible light, resulting in rare report for NiO in overall water splitting. Bismuth vanadate with monoclinic scheelite structure (m-BiVO4) is considered as a promising candidate in water splitting for O2 evolution or decomposition of pollutant under visible light [18–20], due to its appropriate band gap (2.4 eV) for visible light absorption and good stability in aqueous solution. Nevertheless, its short life-times and high recombination rate of the photogenerated charge carries under visible light restrict its further development [21].

Carbon dots (CDs) with chemical stability, low cost, unique photo-induced electron transfer and electron reservoir properties have been studied in many fields, including photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, biosensors and imaging [22,23]. The abundant surface functional moieties (such as OH, CO, COOH) make CDs the fascinating nanomaterial, whose properties can be controllably tuned in the synthesis process. More importantly, the CDs with electron acceptor/donor nature could serve as the electron mediator to boost the electron transfer efficiency [24]. With these excellent properties, CDs can serve as an efficient solid-electron mediator, which is low-cost, environmentally friendly and stable. Therefore, an all solid-state Z-scheme system in combination of NiO, CDs and BiVO4 might be an ideal and effective photocatalyst for visible light-driven overall water splitting.

Herein, we have designed and fabricated the NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites as all solid-state Z-scheme photocatalysts for visible-light-driven overall water splitting without any scavenger. In this system, NiO and BiVO4 can separately work as H2 evolution and O2 evolution photocatalysts, while CDs serve as the solid electron mediator. The exquisite NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites with the CDs concentration in the composite of 5 wt% and the mass ratio of NiO to BiVO4 of 3 show improved photoactivity and durability with optimal gases production rates to be 1.21 μmol∙h−1 (H2) and 0.60 μmol∙h−1 (O2), where the stoichiometry ratio of H2 and O2 was estimated to be 2.02: 1. The favorable photocatalytic activity of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites may be in virtue of the effective separation and transfer of photo-induced charge carriers with addition of CDs, which largely restrain the recombination rate of electrons and holes for water splitting.

Two 99.99% graphite rods were used as carbon sources to obtain CDs nanoparticles by a common electrolytic procedure [25]. In a typical procedure, two graphite rods (d = 6.15 mm, L = 15 cm) were cleaned by ultrasonic in deionized water and ethanol each for 15 min. After washing for 3 times, the neat rods were employed both as anode and cathode respectively in ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm−1, 1000 mL). The DC power supply with voltage of 30 V was applied to these two electrodes and the electrolytic process was kept for two weeks under continuously magnetic stirring. A dark solution was gradually formed during the electro-corrosion of the graphite rods. The resultant solution was then filtered with a slow-speed quantitative filter paper, following with centrifuging at 8000 rpm for 15 min to remove the precipitated graphite oxide and big graphite particles. After that, the CDs powder was acquired cryodesiccation of the CDs aqueous solution.

Typically, 2 g of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O was completely dissolved in 50 mL ultrapure water. Then, NH3·H2O solution was dropped into the above solution till the total precipitation of Ni2+. After the centrifugal and washing process, the green powder of Ni(OH)2 was obtained.

BiVO4 was synthesized through facile hydrothermal method. In detail, 12 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 12 mmol NH4VO3 were respectively dissolved in 50 mL of 2 M HNO3. Then, two solutions were mixed with each other to form a stable yellow suspension. After that, 1 M NH3·H2O solution was dropped into the yellow suspension to adjust the pH value of 7 with sustained stirring for 30 min. Then, the resultant mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 48 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the yellow powder of BiVO4 was obtained by centrifugation, washed with water as well as ethanol several times and then dried in a vacuum drying oven.

0.15 g of Ni(OH)2, 0.05 g of BiVO4 and 10 mg CDs powder were grounded for 15 min to obtain the homogenous mixture. Then, the mixture was added into a porcelain boat, which was put into a tubular furnace and kept at 450 °C for 1.5 h under vacuum atmosphere. After cooling down to room temperature, the resultant powder was collected. Then, the product was washed by ethanol and deionized water for several times to remove unexpected impurities. After drying in a vacuum drying oven, the catalysts denoted as NiO/CDs/BiVO4 were obtained. For comparison, different quantities of CDs (0, 1, 5, 20, 30 mg) were added into the composites with the mass ratio of NiO-BiVO4 being 3. After that, the mass ratio of NiO-BiVO4 was adjusted (0, 0.33, 1, 4, 5) in the composites while the amount of CDs (10 mg) was remained unchanged.

All the regents are purchased from Sigma and Adamas-beta® without further purification. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was carried out to characterize the crystal structure of the as-prepared products by using a PIXcel3D X-ray diffractometer (Empyrean, Holland Panalytical) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm). The scanning electron microscope (SEM) was applied to characterize the surface morphology and element contents of the samples. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were measured by using a FEI-Tecnai F20 transmission electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of the samples was acquired from a Hyperion spectrophotometer (Bruker) at the scan range of 400–4000 cm−1. The Raman spectrum was recorded on an HR 800 Raman spectrometer (J Y, France) equipped with a synapse CCD detector and a confocal Olympus microscope with a 633 nm He–Ne laser as an incident light. UV/VIS/NIR spectrophotometer (Lambda 750, Perkinelmer) was employed to acquire the UV–vis absorption spectra. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted on a KRATOS Axis ultra-DLD X-ray photo-electron spectroscope with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (hυ = 1283.3 eV). Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) measurements were performed with He Ⅰ (21.22 eV) as the monochromatic light source and a total instrumental energy resolution of 100 meV. The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum was recorded on a Horiba Jobin Yvon (Fluoro Max-4) luminescence spectrometer. All the electrochemical measurements were conducted on a CHI 920C workstation (CH Instruments, Shanghai, China), using a standard three-electrode system, where a platinum wire is used as the counter electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode and a glass carbon (GC) electrode as working electrode. Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) measurements were carried out at open circuit potential as well, with a frequency range from 1 MHz to 0.01 Hz and an AC voltage amplitude of 5 mV in ultrapure water.

The photocatalytic property of the as-prepared samples was evaluated using (300 W Xe-lamp, PLS-SXE 300, Beijing Trusttech Co. Ltd, China), a cut-off filter was used to get visible light (λ ≥ 420 nm). The absorption curve of the visible light cut-off is also given in Fig. S1. No sacrificial agents or cocatalysts were needed. In a typical process, 10 mg of the as-prepared sample was added into 25 mL ultrapure water, and ultrasonic dispersion was carried out for at least 10 min to get a homogenous mixture. Then the homogenous dispersion was transferred into a glass photoreactor (equipped with a quartz window at the bottom for illumination), which was sealed with a rubber plug. The reacting system was degassed for 15 min with nitrogen to completely remove dissolved O2 from the air. Then the photoreactor was located into a multichannel photochemical reaction system. To detect the gas production, a gas chromatograph (GC-7900) setup with a 5 Å molecular sieves column and a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) was utilized. Nitrogen gas was used as the carrier gas with the flow rate of 30 mL·min−1.

A 420 nm band-pass filter was used to measure the AQE for H2 evolution. The average intensity of light source here is determined to be 2.84 mW·cm−2 by ILT 950 spectroradiometer and the irradiation area is controlled as 4.27 cm2. The number of incident photons (N) was calculated to be 1.11 × 1021 by Eq. (1) [26] listed below. The amount of H2 produced in 24 h for NiO/CDs/BiVO4 as a photocatalyst was 22.72 µmol. The AQE of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 was 1.24% at λ = 420 nm as calculated by Eq. (2). In addition, different wavelengths (460, 500 and 600 nm) were calculated by the same method.
(1)
N =
E λ
hc
=
2.84 ×
10
- 3
× 4.27 × 24 × 3600 × 420 ×
10
- 9
6.626 ×
10
- 34
× 3 ×
10
8
= 2.21 ×
10 21
(2)
AQE =
2 × the
number
of
evolved
H 2
molecules
the
number
of
incident
photons
× 100 % =
2 × 22.72 ×
10
- 6
× 6.02 ×
10
23
2.21 ×
10
21
× 100 % = 1.24 % =
2 × 22.72 ×
10
- 6
× 6.02 ×
10
23
2.21 ×
10
21
× 100 % = 1.24 %

The electron transfer number was determined via rotating disk-ring electrodes (RRDE) testing system (RRDE-3A, ALS Co., Ltd). RRDE experiments were carried out in ultrapure water (N2 saturated) with a scan rate of 10 mV·s−1 and a rotating speed of 1600 rpm. The disk potential was set at open circuit potential to avoid electrochemical catalysis process for water oxidation. The ring potential was kept at 0.9 V vs. SCE, which can oxidize the generated H2O2 from the disk into O2. All the process and data were recorded by a CHI 920C electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments, Shanghai, China).

The electron transfer number (n) was evaluated according to the Eq. (3) [27]:
(3)
n =
4
I d
I d
+
I r
/ N

In the formula,
I d
and
I r
represent the disk and ring current, respectively, while N is the RRDE collection efficiency determined to be 0.24.

The Z-scheme NiO/CDs/BiVO4 photocatalysts were obtained by annealing in a tube furnace under vacuum. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was employed to characterize the phase purity and crystallinity of the as-prepared samples. Fig. 1 a shows XRD patterns of NiO, BiVO4 and NiO/CDs/BiVO4, which reveal that the synthesized samples are highly crystalline. The XRD patterns from NiO and BiVO4 can be referred to the structures of cubic NiO (JCPDS No. 1-1239) and monoclinic BiVO4 (JCPDS No. 14-0688), respectively. XRD pattern of CDs in Fig. S2b shows two broad peaks at approximately 26˚and 43˚, which can be attributed to the (0 0 2) and (1 0 0) planes of graphitic carbon [28]. All diffraction peaks in XRD pattern of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites are correspond with those of the cubic NiO and monoclinic BiVO4, meanwhile the positions of these peaks remain unchanged. However, no obvious characteristic peak of CDs can be observed in the XRD pattern of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites, due to the relatively low diffraction peak intensity of CDs [29]. The XRD data suggest the highly crystalline NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites without other impurities were successfully synthesized.

The FTIR spectra of NiO, BiVO4, CDs and NiO/CDs/BiVO4 are displayed in Fig. 1b, which was applied to examine the functional groups in the catalysts. As can be seen, four spectra all have the same peak situated at 3440 cm−1, indicating the stretching vibrations of O–H. The three main characteristic peaks at 1046, 1399 and 1628 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of CDs can be assigned to the stretching vibration of COC, COO and CC/CO [25], respectively. The FTIR spectrum of pure NiO shows the strong absorption bond of Ni-O at 440 cm−1 [30,31]. For BiVO4, the main absorption bands at about 695–890 cm−1 can be attributed to υ1(VO4) and υ3(VO4) [32]. However, the relatively weak absorption bonds at 1629 and 1410 cm−1 can also be observed for pure NiO and BiVO4, which are attributed to hydroxyl groups and carbonates [32,33]. The carbon impurity in the pure NiO and BiVO4 is mainly due to the adsorption of CO2 or residual ethanol during the preparation of samples. All absorption peaks in the FTIR spectrum of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites are originated from the superposed infrared absorption of NiO, BiVO4 and CDs.

Fig. 1c displays the Raman spectra of samples from 200 to 2000 cm−1 region. There are two prominent peaks located at 1333 and 1600 cm−1 in the Raman spectrum of CDs, which are ascribed to the typical D band (defects of the CDs) and G band (E2g mode of the graphite) [25,34]. In the Raman spectrum of NiO, the sharp peak centered at 503 cm−1 and the inconspicuous broad one at 1054 cm−1 are the first- (1P) and second-order phonon (2P) scattering [35], respectively. Five Raman bands at around 209, 329, 365, 713, and 830 cm−1 are observed in the Raman spectrum of BiVO4, which are ascribed to the typical vibrational bands of monoclinic phase BiVO4 [36,37]. The red curve shown in Fig. 1c is the Raman spectrum of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites, which exhibits the conjunctively characteristic Raman bands of NiO, BiVO4 and CDs.

The morphological features of the as-prepared samples were investigated by transmission electron microscope (TEM). Fig. 2 shows the typical TEM and corresponding high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of NiO, BiVO4 and NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites. The TEM image of mono-dispersed CDs with uniform particle size of 4 nm is provided in Fig. S2a and the inserted HRTEM image shows the lattice distance of 0.21 nm, which assigns to the (1 0 0) spacing of graphitic carbon [38]. As shown in Fig. 2a, the NiO nanoparticles reveal a flake-like morphology. The HRTEM image of NiO in Fig. 2d displays the obvious lattice fringes with the distance of 0.209 nm, which can be attributed to the (2 0 0) crystal plane of cubic NiO [39]. Fig. 2b shows that the BiVO4 has irregular shape and the corresponding lattice space of 0.31 nm (Fig. 2e) can be ascribed to the (−1 2 1) planes in monoclinic BiVO4 [40]. The TEM image of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites is given in Fig. 2c, which reveals that the BiVO4 particles are loaded on the NiO nanoparticles. The representative HRTEM image of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 is illustrated in Fig. 2f, where the lattice spaces of 0.209, 0.21, 0.31 nm correspond to the (2 0 0) facet of NiO, the (1 0 0) spacing of graphitic carbon and the (−1 2 1) plane of BiVO4 [38–40], respectively. These results are consistent with SEM results shown in Fig. S3. The good contact between NiO, CDs and BiVO4 is beneficial to the expedited electron transfer rate and improved photocatalytic performance of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 Z-scheme composites.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted to clarify the surface element contents and valence states of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites. The XPS full spectrum displayed in Fig. 3 a reveals the binding energy levels of C, O, Ni, Bi and V elements on the surface of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 without any other impurities, which are well identical with the HAADF-STEM and EDX elemental mapping images results shown in Fig. S4. The C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3b) can be deconvoluted into three individual peaks located at 284.7, 286.3, and 288.3 eV, corresponding to CC, CO, and CO bonds [25,28], respectively. Fig. 3c shows the deconvoluted O 1 s spectrum of NiO/CDs/BiVO4, where the peaks at 529.4, 531.1 and 532.4 eV are attributed to surface lattice oxygen (Olatt) in monoclinic BiVO4, NiO bond and OH from CDs or absorbed water [41–44]. The Ni 2p region (Fig. 3d) are fitted into five discernible features: Ni 2p3/2 (853.9 and 855.7 eV) and Ni 2p1/2 (872.7 eV) spin–orbit levels of NiO [42], whilst two satellite peaks at 861.1 and 879.6 eV, respectively. In the Bi 4f spectrum (Fig. 3e), two prominent peaks (159.1 and 164.4 eV) with the split energy of 5.3 eV are observed corresponding to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, which indicates Bi3+ valence state in monoclinic BiVO4. Fig. 3f shows the core level spectrum of V 2p, which comprises two binding energies at 516.9 and 524.4 eV assigned to V 2p3/2 and V 2p1/2 [43]. Therefore, the analysis of XPS results above further demonstrates the synthesis of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 Z-scheme composites without other impurities.

Fig. 4 a shows the ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra of NiO, BiVO4 and NiO/CDs/BiVO4, which are used to investigate the optical properties of as-prepared catalysts. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of NiO shows the relatively weak absorption during the wavelength range of 420–580 nm, which can be attributed to the sub-band transition (Ni 3d8 → 3d9) of NiO for visible light absorption [45]. After the incorporation of BiVO4 and CDs, the NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites show the enhanced light absorption through the entirely detective wavelength range compared to those of pure NiO and BiVO4. The incorporation of CDs into pure NiO or BiVO4 can also enhance the UV–vis absorption, which is shown in Fig. S5. The Tauc plot derived from the UV–Vis spectrum can be used to estimate the optical band gap of a semiconductor. According to the following Eq. (4) [46]:
(4)
α h ν
= A
h ν -
E g
n / 2

Where the parameters α, h, ν, A and Eg are absorption coefficient, Planck’s constant, frequency of light, factor of proportionality and band energy, respectively. The inset figure in Fig. 4a shows a good linear fit both for NiO and BiVO4 with n = 1, indicating the direct band gaps of NiO and BiVO4. Accordingly, the band gap energies of NiO and BiVO4 were calculated to be 2.76 and 2.36 eV, respectively.

With the exception of optimal band gap energy, the appropriate band positions relative to the electrochemical potentials E° (H+/H2) and E° (O2/H2O) are also vital to realize overall water splitting under visible light [5].The UPS spectra of NiO and BiVO4 are shown in Fig. 4b and c, which are used to determine the ionization potential [equivalent to the valence band energy (Ev)] of a semiconductor [28,46]. The Ev values of NiO and BiVO4 are thus calculated to be 6.38 and 7.03 eV by subtracting the width of the He I UPS spectrum from the exciting energy (21.22 eV). According to the formula Ec = Ev − Eg, the corresponding Ec values of NiO and BiVO4 are 3.62 and 4.67 eV, respectively. The Ev and Ec values of NiO and BiVO4 in electron volts can be converted into electrochemical energy potentials in volts regarding to the reference standard (0 V vs. RHE equals to –4.44 eV vs. vacuum level). Therefore, the Ev and Ec values of NiO in volts are 1.94 and −0.82 V (vs. RHE), while those of BiVO4 in volts are 2.59 and 0.23 V. Fig. 4d shows the band structure diagrams of NiO and BiVO4. The Ec value of NiO is more negative than the reduction potential of H+/H2, which can be responsible for the hydrogen evolution. Both Ev and Ec values of BiVO4 are more positive than reduction potential of H+/H2. As a result, BiVO4 can be the active site for the oxygen evolution in this Z-scheme system. Based on their electronic structure, the as-synthesized NiO/CDs/BiVO4 Z-scheme photocatalysts can meet the basic requirements for water splitting under visible light illumination.

The amount of gases production can be used to estimate the photocatalytic properties of the synthetic photocatalysts. Fig. 5 a shows the production of H2 and O2 catalyzed by NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites under visible light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm), where the optimal CDs concentration is 0.05 gCDs/gcatalyst. It is noteworthy that the water splitting system merely contains the photocatalysts and pure water without sacrificial agents or any other cocatalyst. The produced H2 and O2 were both quantified by gas chromatography (GC) using N2 as the carrying gas. The highest H2 production rate was calculated to be 1.21 μmol·h−1 and the corresponding O2 evolution rate was 0.60 μmol·h−1. The stoichiometry ratio of H2 and O2 was figured out to be 2.02: 1, indicating that the NiO/CDs/BiVO4 photocatalysts are capable of complete splitting water under irradiation of visible light. The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 under different light wavelength is measured as is depicted in Fig. S6. The highest AQE of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 photocatalysts can be calculated to be 1.24% at λ = 420 nm for overall water splitting. The cyclic stability experiment was performed to evaluate the photocatalytic stability of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites. Fig. 5b shows that the NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites exhibit stably catalytic activity for 8 cyclic experiments (Each time is 12 h irradiation under visible light). The washing process during the cyclic experiments might result in slight weight loss of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites [25,46], which causes 4% deduction of hydrogen production after 8th cycle.

CDs as the solid state electron mediator are the key factor to improve photocatalytic efficiency of the NiO/CDs/BiVO4 Z-scheme composites. Different amounts of CDs were added into the composites (Fig. 5c) for comparison, while the mass ratio of NiO-BiVO4 is maintained as 3. Little amount of hydrogen (0.04 μmol·h−1) was detected without CDs in the NiO/BiVO4 composites, which reveals that NiO and BiVO4 can be constructed as the Z-scheme photocatalyst despite the low photoactivity under visible light. With the concentration of CDs increasing to 0.05 gCDs/gcatalyst in the NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites, the optimal hydrogen production rate (1.21 μmol·h−1) is 30 times higher than that of the NiO/BiVO4 composites. The dramatically improved catalytic activity of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 could be attributed to high separation efficiency of charge carries, where CDs can serve as the solid-state electron mediator. Unfortunately, when the CDs concentration is more than 0.05 gCDs/gcatalyst, the hydrogen production decreased, which may result from the hindering of active sites as well as the energy loss by excessive CDs [28].

The influence of proportion of NiO and BiVO4 on the catalytic activity of Z-scheme NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites was also discussed with further experiments, whilst the CDs concentration was remained as 5 wt%. As is shown in Fig. 5d, there is no hydrogen produced without NiO (CDs/BiVO4), which can be attributed to the lower conduction band position of pure BiVO4 compared to H+/H2 potential [21]. Notably, the conduction band position of BiVO4 can’t be changed even with the addition of CDs. The maximum hydrogen production rate reaches to 1.21 μmol·h−1, when the mass ratio of NiO-BiVO4 is 3. More NiO in the NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites seems to lower the utilization of visible light leading to the decreased hydrogen production and pure NiO or CDs/NiO cannot produce H2 under the same condition (Fig. S7), which means that the CDs can’t lower the recombination rate of charge carriers in NiO. Based on the data above, BiVO4 can effectively depress the combination of photo-induced charge carriers in NiO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the photocatalytic mechanism and process
ce:section.ce:para: The curves of transient photocurrent vs. time (i-t) of NiO, BiVO4 and NiO/CDs/BiVO4 under open circuit potential are displayed in Fig. 6 a, where the period of light on/off (λ ≥ 420 nm) is maintained as 50 s. The pure NiO shows a weak photoresponse under the illumination of visible light, which testifies the Ni 3d8 to 3d9 sub-band transition of NiO despite the fast hole-electron recombination. It can be also observed that the NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites show a great photoresponse as well as the tardy photocurrent reduction compared to those of pure NiO and BiVO4, revealing the higher charge separation efficiency of the as-synthesized Z-scheme catalysts [24]. The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of NiO, BiVO4 and NiO/CDs/BiVO4 are shown in Fig. 6b, respectively. After the formation of Z-scheme phocatalysts composed with NiO, CDs and BiVO4, NiO/CDs/BiVO4 has the smallest radius of semicircle, revealing the smallest charge transfer resistance (Rct) and also the fastest electron transfer speed [47]. The PL spectra of NiO, BiVO4 and NiO/CDs/BiVO4 were recorded under the excitation wavelength of 365 nm, which are shown in Fig. 6c. The greatly reduction of the PL intensity of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites reveals highly efficient charge carriers separation [48], compared to those of NiO and BiVO4. These results all confirm the fast electron transfer rate and high electron-hole pair separation efficiency in NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites, which lead to highly photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared catalysts. Fig. 6d shows the result of RRDE collection experiment the electron transfer number (n) for NiO/CDs/BiVO4 to investigate the character of the water splitting process. After the irradiation of visible light (λ ≥ 420 nm), there is a relatively strong saltation in the disk current while no variability in the ring current. The electron transfer number can be derived from the Eq. (3) as is described in experimental section. Hence the electron transfer number of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 was determined to be about 4, which means the water splitting process catalyzed by NiO/CDs/BiVO4 Z-scheme photocatalysts undergoing the four-electron pathway.

On the basis of all data analysis above, a conceivable mechanism is proposed for Z-scheme NiO/CDs/BiVO4 photocatalysts throughout the whole overall water splitting process, which is depicted in Fig. 7 . Both NiO and BiVO4 can absorb visible light to generate electron-hole pairs. Additionally, the sub-band transition (Ni 3d8 → 3d9) of NiO can promote the utilization of visible light wavelength larger than 450 nm. The holes produced in the valence band of BiVO4 will oxide H2O to produce O2. The photo-induced electrons in the conduction band of NiO are responsible for the reduction of H2O into H2. Meanwhile, the excited electrons can be transferred from the conduction band of BiVO4 to the valence band of NiO to combine with the photo-induced holes with the help of CDs as the solid electron mediator. The improved catalytic performance of NiO/CDs/BiVO4 photocatalysts can be attributed to the fast electron transfer rate and efficient charge separation, which prolongate the lifespan of photo-induced charge carriers for overall water splitting.

In this work, we demonstrate the designed NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites as effective all-solid-state Z-scheme photocatalysts for visible light-driven overall water splitting without any scavenger. The NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites show improved photoactivity with the optimal gases production rates to be 1.21 μmol∙h−1 (H2) and 0.60 μmol∙h−1 (O2), where the stoichiometry ratio of H2 and O2 was estimated to be 2.02: 1. The as-prepared catalysts also possess good durability for eight cyclic process and maximum AQE of 1.24% at 420 nm for overall water splitting. In this system, NiO and BiVO4 are responsible for H2 evolution and O2 evolution, respectively, while the CDs can serve as the solid electron mediator. The separation efficiency and transfer property of photo-induced charge carriers in NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites are greatly expedited after the incorporation of CDs, which in turn enhance the photocatalytic activity of the composites. Our work utilizes the sub-band transition of a wide band gap semiconductor to realize overall water splitting under visible light, which may provide a new avenue to exploit this kind of material to fabricate highly efficient photocatalyts.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51725204, 51572179, 21471106, 21771132, 21501126), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20161216), the 111 project and a project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2018.10.005.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
